title: Project Management Software Pricing Guide 2026
description: A comprehensive pricing guide for project management software in 2026 comparing Asana, Monday.com, Jira, and Linear with detailed cost analysis, hidden fees, and ROI insights.

sections.title: Overview of Project Management Pricing Models
sections.body: Project management software pricing in 2026 is characterized by a diverse range of models that reflect the varying needs of different team types, from small creative teams to large engineering organizations. Unlike many SaaS categories with standardized pricing approaches, project management tools exhibit significant variation in how they charge, creating a landscape where the same team size can face dramatically different costs depending on the platform chosen. The primary pricing models in project management are per-user monthly subscriptions, flat-rate plans with user tiers, free tiers with paid upgrades, and usage-based pricing for storage or automation runs. Per-user pricing is the most common model, used by Asana, Monday.com, Jira, and Linear. Under this model, each team member requires a paid license, and the monthly cost scales linearly with team size. This model is straightforward but can become expensive for large teams with many viewers or stakeholders who need only read access to projects. Feature-tiered pricing adds a second dimension, where higher plan levels unlock advanced capabilities like timeline views, goals and OKRs, portfolio management, custom fields, and advanced reporting. Most platforms offer three to four tiers, with the mid-tier being the most popular for professional teams. The per-user cost typically increases from $8-14 per user per month at the basic tier to $16-30 per user per month at the premium tier, representing a 2-3x increase in per-user cost for advanced features. Free tiers are widely available in project management software but vary significantly in generosity. Asana offers a free tier for up to 10 users with basic project management features. Monday.com offers a free tier for up to 2 users. Jira offers a free tier for up to 10 users. Linear offers a free tier for up to 10 users. The free tiers are sufficient for small teams with basic needs but lack advanced features like timeline views, automation, and reporting that professional teams require. A critical distinction in project management pricing is between work management platforms and software development tools. Asana and Monday.com are general work management platforms designed for all team types. Jira and Linear are specialized for software development teams, with features for issue tracking, sprint planning, and engineering workflows. This specialization affects pricing, with development-focused tools typically offering different feature sets at each price point. Understanding these pricing models is essential for selecting a platform that matches your team's size, workflow complexity, and budget. The same 20-person team can pay $2,000 per year on a basic plan or $15,000 per year on a premium plan depending on the platform and tier selected.

sections.title: Asana Pricing Breakdown
sections.body: Asana is one of the most widely used project management platforms, known for its intuitive interface and flexibility across team types. Asana offers four pricing tiers that serve teams from small startups to large enterprises. The Free tier includes core project management features for up to 10 users. Free features include unlimited projects, tasks, and conversations, list, board, and calendar views, basic search and reporting, and mobile app access. The Free tier does not include timeline view, workflows, goals, or portfolio management. The Free tier is appropriate for very small teams managing simple projects, but the 10-user limit and missing features force growing teams to upgrade. The Starter plan costs $13.50 per user per month when billed annually, or $15.50 per user per month when billed monthly. Starter includes everything in Free plus timeline view for visual project planning, Gantt chart functionality for dependency management, workflows with rule-based automation, private teams and projects for secure collaboration, admin console for user management, and priority support. The Starter plan is Asana's most popular tier, providing sufficient features for most professional teams managing complex projects. The Advanced plan costs $30.50 per user per month when billed annually, or $35.50 per user per month when billed monthly. Advanced adds goals and OKRs with progress tracking and alignment, portfolio management with cross-project visibility, time tracking integrations, custom rules builder for advanced automation, approvals for workflow sign-offs, and workload view for resource management. The Advanced plan is appropriate for organizations managing multiple projects with portfolio-level visibility requirements. The Enterprise plan offers custom pricing for organizations with advanced security, compliance, and administrative needs. Enterprise includes everything in Advanced plus SAML-based single sign-on, data export and deletion tools, custom branding and white-labeling, priority support with dedicated customer success manager, advanced admin controls with audit logs, and custom integrations through Asana's API. Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted based on the number of users, required features, and contract length. Asana offers a 30-day free trial of the Starter or Advanced plan with no credit card required. The trial provides full access to all features in the selected plan. Asana also offers discounts for non-profit organizations and educational institutions, typically 50% off the subscription price. Annual billing provides a 15% discount compared to monthly billing on all paid plans. A notable cost consideration with Asana is that guests who are added to individual tasks or projects require a full paid license if they need more than commenting permissions. This means external collaborators, contractors, or stakeholders who need to create or edit tasks require a paid seat, which can significantly increase costs for organizations that work extensively with external partners.

sections.title: Monday.com Pricing Breakdown
sections.body: Monday.com has grown rapidly by offering a highly visual, customizable work operating system that serves teams across departments. Its pricing is structured around five tiers with increasing feature depth. The Free tier includes basic features for up to 2 users. Free features include unlimited boards, 200+ templates, column types including text, numbers, dates, and dropdowns, and mobile app access. The Free tier does not include timeline, Gantt, or calendar views, automation, or integrations. The 2-user limit makes the Free tier suitable only for solopreneurs or very small teams evaluating the platform. The Basic plan costs $12 per user per month when billed annually, or $14 per user per month when billed monthly. Basic adds unlimited viewers, column types for file sharing and location, and iOS and Android mobile apps. Basic does not include automation, integrations, timeline view, or Gantt view. The Basic plan is limited compared to competitors at similar price points and is best suited for teams that need simple board-based project tracking. The Standard plan costs $14 per user per month when billed annually, or $16 per user per month when billed monthly. Standard adds timeline and Gantt views for visual project planning, calendar view for deadline tracking, 250 automation and integration actions per month, guest access for external collaborators, and 5 GB of storage. Standard is the entry point for teams that need automation and visual planning tools. The Pro plan costs $24 per user per month when billed annually, or $28 per user per month when billed monthly. Pro adds private boards for confidential projects, 25,000 automation and integration actions per month, time tracking for billable hour management, formula column for calculated fields, dependencies between tasks, and 20 GB of storage. Pro is Monday.com's most popular tier for professional teams. The Enterprise plan offers custom pricing for organizations with advanced requirements. Enterprise includes everything in Pro plus enterprise-scale automation and integration limits, advanced reporting with dashboard analytics, multi-level permissions and hierarchy management, premium support with dedicated account manager, and security features including SOC 2 compliance and SAML-based SSO. Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted. Monday.com offers a 14-day free trial of any paid plan with no credit card required. The trial provides full access to the selected plan's features. Monday.com also offers volume discounts for 50+ users, with typical discounts ranging from 10-20% off the per-user price. Annual billing provides a 15% discount compared to monthly billing. A significant cost consideration with Monday.com is the automation and integration action limits on the Standard and Pro plans. The Standard plan includes only 250 actions per month, which is extremely restrictive for teams that rely on automation for task assignments, status updates, and notification workflows. Teams that use automation heavily may find themselves forced to the Pro plan at $24 per user per month, which represents a 70% price increase over the Standard plan for automation capacity alone. Monday.com's per-user pricing at the Pro tier ($24/user/mo) is higher than Asana Starter ($13.50/user/mo) and Jira Standard ($8.15/user/mo), making it one of the more expensive options at the mid-tier for teams that need automation and timeline features.

sections.title: Jira Pricing Breakdown
sections.body: Jira by Atlassian is the dominant project management platform for software development teams, offering purpose-built features for issue tracking, agile development, and DevOps integration. Jira's pricing is structured around the number of users and the feature tier, with significant discounts for smaller teams. The Free tier supports up to 10 users with core Jira features including Scrum and Kanban boards, backlog management, 2 GB of storage, basic reporting, and community support. The Free tier does not include advanced roadmaps, sandbox environments, or Atlassian Intelligence features. The Free tier is surprisingly generous and sufficient for many small development teams. The Standard plan costs $8.15 per user per month when billed annually, or $10.50 per user per month when billed monthly. Standard adds advanced roadmaps for cross-project planning, 250 GB of storage, project-level permissions and configuration, audit logs, and standard ticket support with 24-hour response time. Standard is the most popular Jira tier for growing development teams. The Premium plan costs $16.25 per user per month when billed annually, or $21 per user per month when billed monthly. Premium adds Atlassian Intelligence with AI-powered search and automation, sandbox environments for safe testing, cross-project planning with dependency management, advanced analytics with real-time reporting, 24/7 support with 1-hour response time on critical issues, and unlimited automation runs. The Premium plan includes 99.9% uptime SLA. The Enterprise plan offers custom pricing for organizations with 801+ users. Enterprise includes everything in Premium plus 99.99% uptime SLA, centralized administration through Atlassian Admin, unlimited sandboxes, 24/7 support with 30-minute response time, and custom contract terms including data residency and compliance requirements. Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted through the Atlassian sales team. Jira also offers several add-on products that affect total cost. Jira Service Management for IT support teams starts at $8.15 per user per month for Standard. Jira Product Discovery for product managers starts at $15 per user per month. Confluence for team documentation starts at $6.05 per user per month for Standard. Jira Align for enterprise agile planning is custom-quoted and typically costs $25-50 per user per month. Atlassian's marketplace offers thousands of add-ons that extend Jira functionality, with most costing $5-50 per month per add-on. A critical cost advantage of Jira is its per-user pricing for software teams. At $8.15 per user per month for Standard, Jira is significantly cheaper than Asana Starter at $13.50 and Monday.com Standard at $14 per user per month. However, Jira's specialization for software development means it is less suitable for non-engineering teams. Organizations that need a single platform for all teams may find that Jira's learning curve and development-focused interface create adoption barriers for marketing, sales, or operations teams. Jira's automation capabilities are notably generous compared to competitors. The Standard plan includes unlimited automation runs, while Monday.com's Standard plan includes only 250 automation actions per month. For teams that rely heavily on automation for task creation, assignment, and status updates, Jira's unlimited automation at the Standard tier provides significant value. Atlassian offers a 30-day free trial of Standard or Premium plans. The trial provides full access with no credit card required. Annual billing provides a 20% discount compared to monthly billing.

sections.title: Linear Pricing Breakdown
sections.body: Linear has gained significant adoption among modern software development teams for its speed, simplicity, and developer-friendly approach to issue tracking and project management. Linear's pricing is straightforward with two primary tiers. The Free tier supports up to 10 users with core issue tracking features. Free features include issue creation and management with customizable workflows, project management with cycles and roadmaps, sprint planning with team velocity tracking, keyboard-first interface optimized for developer workflow, GitHub and GitLab integration for automatic issue status updates, and Slack integration for real-time notifications. The Free tier is generous and provides sufficient features for small development teams. The Standard plan costs $12 per user per month when billed annually, or $14 per user per month when billed monthly. Standard adds all features including admin controls for team management, priority support with faster response times, file uploads up to 5 MB per attachment, attachment storage of 10 GB, and API access with higher rate limits. Standard is designed for growing teams that need administrative control and support. The Enterprise plan offers custom pricing for organizations with advanced security, compliance, and administrative requirements. Enterprise includes everything in Standard plus SAML-based single sign-on, SCIM provisioning for user management, audit logs for security monitoring, dedicated support with SLAs, and custom contract terms. Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted through Linear's sales team. Linear's pricing is notable for its simplicity. There are no feature gates between Free and Standard beyond admin controls, support priority, and storage limits. This means a team on the Standard plan has access to the same core functionality as a team on an Enterprise plan. This approach contrasts sharply with competitors that gate advanced features like roadmaps, timelines, and automation behind higher-priced tiers. However, Linear's feature set is also intentionally narrower than competitors. Linear focuses specifically on software development workflows and does not offer the broad work management capabilities of Asana or Monday.com. Linear does not include portfolio management, goals and OKRs, time tracking, or custom field types beyond what is needed for issue tracking. These limitations make Linear less suitable for non-engineering teams or organizations that need comprehensive work management. A key cost advantage of Linear is the simplicity and transparency of its pricing. There are no hidden automation limits, storage caps on lower tiers that force upgrades, or guest license costs. The Free tier for up to 10 users is genuinely functional for small development teams, and the Standard plan at $12 per user per month is competitively priced against Jira Standard at $8.15 per user per month and Asana Starter at $13.50 per user per month. Linear offers a 14-day free trial of the Standard plan with no credit card required. The trial provides full access to all Standard features. Linear does not offer annual billing discounts beyond the listed annual pricing. Linear has announced plans to introduce team-level billing and additional enterprise features, but the current pricing structure remains one of the simplest in the project management category. For small to mid-sized software development teams, Linear's combination of competitive pricing, unlimited features across tiers, and exceptional user experience makes it a compelling alternative to Jira despite the narrower feature set.

sections.title: Project Management Pricing Comparison Table
sections.body: A side-by-side comparison of pricing across the major project management platforms, showing per-user costs and key differentiators.

sections.title: Hidden Costs in Project Management Software Pricing
sections.body: Project management software carries several categories of hidden costs that can significantly increase the total expense beyond the headline per-user pricing. Understanding these costs before selecting a platform prevents budget overruns and ensures accurate vendor comparison.
items: Guest and external collaborator costs: Many platforms charge full per-user license fees for external collaborators, contractors, or clients who need more than read-only access. Asana requires a full paid license for guests who need to create or edit tasks. For organizations working with multiple external partners, these costs can add 20-50% to the total license count.

Automation and integration limits: Monday.com's Standard plan includes only 250 automation actions per month, which is quickly consumed by task assignments, status updates, and notifications. Teams needing moderate automation are forced to the Pro plan at $24/user/mo, a 70% increase over Standard. These usage-based limits can force costly plan upgrades.

Storage limits and file attachment costs: Most project management platforms cap storage at the lower tiers. Jira Free includes 2 GB. Asana Starter includes 100 GB. Exceeding storage limits requires plan upgrades or additional storage purchases. Teams that share design files, documents, or screenshots within projects can exhaust storage within months.

Premium support costs: Standard support on most platforms is email-only with 24-48 hour response times. Teams that need faster response or phone support must purchase premium support plans, typically adding 15-25% to the subscription cost. Priority support is often reserved for Enterprise tiers.

Integration and add-on marketplace costs: While basic integrations are included, premium integrations and marketplace add-ons carry separate subscription costs. Jira's marketplace has thousands of add-ons, most costing $5-50 per month. A team using 5 paid add-ons adds $25-250/month to the Jira subscription cost.

Security and compliance add-ons: SAML single sign-on, enforced two-factor authentication, and audit logs are typically reserved for Enterprise tiers. Organizations with security compliance requirements are forced to Enterprise pricing regardless of team size. Asana and Monday.com gate SSO behind Enterprise plans.

Migration and data export costs: Switching between project management platforms requires data migration, which is technically challenging due to differences in data models. Professional migration services cost $2,000 to $10,000 depending on data volume and complexity. Some platforms charge for data export through their API in bulk.

Custom development and API integration costs: Connecting project management platforms to internal tools or custom workflows requires API development. Developer time for custom integrations costs $5,000 to $25,000 per integration. Middleware platforms like Zapier add $20-100/month for complex multi-step workflows.

Training and adoption costs: Project management tool adoption requires team training and change management. Comprehensive training programs cost $500 to $5,000 per team. Internal adoption efforts require 5-20 hours per team member during rollout. Low adoption rates directly reduce ROI regardless of platform features.

Over-provisioning licenses: Many organizations buy licenses for all team members including managers and stakeholders who need only read access. Per-user pricing models create disincentives to invite viewers, leading to information silos. Some platforms offer free viewer accounts, but these are limited or unavailable on lower tiers.

sections.title: Enterprise Project Management Pricing
sections.body: Enterprise project management pricing addresses the needs of large organizations with complex project portfolios, multiple teams, and advanced security and compliance requirements. Enterprise pricing in this category is almost entirely custom-quoted and typically ranges from $20,000 to $500,000 annually depending on the number of users, required features, and deployment complexity. Asana Enterprise is custom-quoted based on organizational requirements. Typical Asana Enterprise deployments for 500+ users range from $30 to $50 per user per month depending on contract length and included services. Asana Enterprise includes SAML SSO, custom branding, advanced admin controls with audit logs, priority support with dedicated customer success manager, and custom integration development support. Annual Asana Enterprise contracts for 500 users typically range from $180,000 to $300,000. Monday.com Enterprise is also custom-quoted. Typical Monday.com Enterprise pricing ranges from $30 to $50 per user per month for organizations with standard requirements, with discounts available for large user counts and multi-year commitments. Monday.com Enterprise includes enterprise-scale automation limits, advanced reporting, multi-level permissions, premium support, and security features. Annual Monday.com Enterprise contracts for 500 users typically range from $120,000 to $250,000. Jira Enterprise for 801+ users is priced through Atlassian's sales team. Atlassian Enterprise pricing is based on the number of users and typically ranges from $20 to $30 per user per month for a comprehensive deployment including Jira Software, Jira Service Management, and Confluence. Enterprise includes 99.99% uptime SLA, centralized administration, unlimited sandboxes, 24/7 support with 30-minute response, and data residency options. Annual Jira Enterprise contracts for 1,000 users typically range from $240,000 to $360,000 for the full Atlassian suite. Linear Enterprise is custom-quoted for organizations with advanced security requirements. Linear Enterprise includes SAML SSO, SCIM provisioning, audit logs, and dedicated support. Linear Enterprise pricing has not been publicly detailed given the platform's relative newness to the enterprise market. Enterprise project management selection should prioritize portfolio management capabilities, cross-project reporting and analytics, security and compliance features, integration with enterprise systems including ERP, HRIS, and CRM, and support for hybrid methodologies across different teams. Enterprise organizations should also evaluate platform scalability, data residency options, and vendor financial stability when selecting an enterprise project management platform. The total cost of enterprise project management includes software licensing, implementation and configuration services, custom integration development, ongoing administration and support, and training and change management. Organizations should budget 1.5 to 2 times the software subscription cost for these additional expenses.

sections.title: Free Plans and Trial Options
sections.body: The project management software market offers some of the most generous free tiers in the SaaS industry, driven by competitive pressure and the desire to drive adoption through team-led purchasing. Understanding the limitations and upgrade paths of these free options is essential for making an informed decision. Asana offers a free tier for up to 10 users with unlimited projects, tasks, and conversations. The free tier includes list, board, and calendar views but excludes timeline view, workflows, goals, and portfolio management. Asana's free tier is one of the most generous, supporting a full team of up to 10 users with functional project management capabilities. Asana also offers a 30-day free trial of the Starter or Advanced plan with no credit card required. Monday.com offers a free tier for up to 2 users with unlimited boards and templates. The 2-user limit makes the free tier very restrictive for team evaluation. Monday.com offers a 14-day free trial of any paid plan with full access to the selected plan's features. The trial requires no credit card. Jira offers a free tier for up to 10 users with Scrum and Kanban boards, backlog management, and basic reporting. Jira's free tier is genuinely functional for small software development teams and includes features that competitors gate behind paid plans, such as roadmaps and sprint planning. Jira offers a 30-day free trial of Standard or Premium plans. Linear offers a free tier for up to 10 users with all core features including cycles, roadmaps, and project management. Linear's free tier is unique in that it includes the full feature set with no feature gates, only limits on admin controls and storage. Linear offers a 14-day free trial of the Standard plan. When evaluating project management platforms through free tiers, focus on five critical factors: team adoption ease and the learning curve for non-technical team members, workflow customization flexibility for your specific processes, reporting and visibility quality for stakeholder communication and status tracking, integration reliability with your existing tool stack including communication, file storage, and development tools, and mobile app functionality for team members who need access on the go. The free trial is also the ideal time to test the platform's collaboration features including comments, file sharing, and real-time updates. These collaboration capabilities significantly affect team adoption and daily workflow integration.

sections.title: ROI Analysis of Project Management Software Investment
sections.body: Project management software return on investment is measured through team productivity gains, project completion rates, reduced meeting time, and improved project visibility. For most organizations, the ROI is substantial, with payback periods measured in weeks. Team productivity represents the most quantifiable ROI component. Studies consistently show that project management software reduces time spent on status updates, task tracking, and coordination by 30-50%. For a team of 20 people with an average fully loaded cost of $100,000 per year, saving 20% of coordination time represents $400,000 in recovered productive capacity per year. Against project management software costs of $20,000 to $50,000 per year for a 20-person team, the productivity ROI is 8:1 to 20:1. Project completion rates improve with structured project management. Teams using project management software complete projects 20-30% faster than teams relying on email, spreadsheets, and meetings. For a team managing 10 projects per year with an average project value of $50,000, a 25% improvement in completion speed translates to $125,000 in accelerated value delivery. Timely project delivery also improves client satisfaction and reduces penalties for late delivery. Meeting time reduction is a significant but often overlooked benefit. Without project management software, teams spend 5-10 hours per week in status meetings, standups, and coordination sessions. With real-time project visibility, these meetings can be reduced by 50-75%. For a 20-person team with an average meeting cost of $50 per person per hour, saving 5 hours of meetings per week represents $5,000 in weekly savings or $250,000 annually. This meeting time reduction alone often justifies the software investment. Improved project visibility and risk management provide additional ROI. Project management software with timeline views and dependency tracking helps identify bottlenecks and risks before they become critical. Early risk identification reduces project delays and cost overruns. For organizations managing projects worth $1 million or more annually, a 5% reduction in cost overruns through better visibility represents $50,000 in savings. The total cost of project management software for a 20-person team on Asana Starter illustrates the ROI calculation. Twenty users at $13.50 per user per month costs $3,240 annually. Against productivity gains of $400,000, project completion improvements of $125,000, and meeting time savings of $250,000, the total benefit is approximately $775,000, representing a 240x return on investment. The payback period is typically 1 to 2 weeks. The most important factor in achieving projected ROI is ensuring team-wide adoption. Project management tools provide value proportional to the percentage of the team actively using them. A platform used by 100% of the team delivers 5-10x the ROI of a platform used by 50% of the team. Investing in onboarding, training, and change management is essential for achieving full adoption and maximum ROI.

sections.title: Total Cost of Ownership Buyer Checklist
sections.body: Use this comprehensive checklist to calculate the true total cost of project management software, accounting for both direct subscription costs and the hidden expenses that affect the overall investment.
items: Per-user monthly subscription cost at the plan tier that meets your feature requirements
Number of paid users including team members, managers, and any external collaborators who need full access
Guest and external collaborator licensing costs and whether they require full paid seats
Automation and integration run limits that may require higher-tier plans for moderate usage
Storage limits per user and additional storage costs if exceeded
Premium support costs if standard support response times are insufficient for your team
Marketplace or add-on subscription costs for essential integrations and extensions
Security features availability including SSO, audit logs, and compliance certifications at your chosen tier
Training and onboarding costs including team training sessions and change management resources
Data migration costs if switching from another project management platform
Annual billing discount percentage compared to monthly billing
Custom development costs for API integrations with your existing tool stack
Multi-year contract discounts and price escalation terms for long-term commitments
Data export and portability capabilities to ensure you are not locked into the platform

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions About Project Management Software Pricing
sections.body: Common questions about project management software pricing that address the key cost considerations and platform selection criteria for teams of all sizes.
items: What is the average cost of project management software per user per month? The average cost ranges from $8 to $30 per user per month depending on the platform and feature tier. Jira Standard at $8.15/user/mo is the cheapest major option. Asana Advanced at $30.50/user/mo is at the high end. Most professional teams spend $12-16/user/mo.

Do project management tools offer free plans for small teams? Yes, most major platforms offer free tiers. Asana and Jira offer free plans for up to 10 users. Linear offers free for up to 10 users. Monday.com offers free for only 2 users. Free tiers are sufficient for very small teams with basic needs.

What is the cheapest project management software for a team of 20? Jira Standard at $8.15/user/mo is the cheapest for a 20-person team at $163/month. Linear Standard at $12/user/mo is $240/month. Asana Starter at $13.50/user/mo is $270/month. Jira is the most cost-effective option for software teams.

Can I use Jira for non-software teams like marketing or HR? Yes, but Jira is optimized for software development workflows. Non-engineering teams often find Jira's interface and terminology less intuitive than general work management tools like Asana or Monday.com. Some organizations use Jira for all teams, but adoption may be lower outside engineering.

Do project management tools charge for viewers who only need read access? Most platforms charge full per-user license fees for all users. Asana and Monday.com do not offer free viewer accounts. Jira offers free viewer access through its Free plan for up to 10 users. Linear does not distinguish between viewers and editors.

What hidden costs should I watch for in project management contracts? Watch for automation limit costs that force upgrades, guest licensing fees for external collaborators, premium support costs, marketplace add-on subscriptions, and SSO feature gates that require enterprise pricing for security features.

How often do project management software prices increase? Annual price increases of 5-10% are standard. Atlassian has historically increased Jira prices every 12-18 months. Asana increased prices in 2024. Linear has not announced significant price increases. Multi-year contracts with price locks protect against increases.

What is the total cost of project management for a 50-person company? A 50-person company should budget $8,000 to $30,000 annually for project management software. A Jira Standard deployment costs approximately $4,900/year. An Asana Starter deployment costs $8,100/year. A Monday.com Pro deployment costs $14,400/year.

Should I choose one platform for all teams or different tools for different departments? A single platform maximizes cross-team visibility and reduces licensing complexity but may sacrifice departmental fit. Many organizations use Jira for engineering and Asana for business teams. The consolidation benefit of a single platform is worth 10-20% in licensing savings.

Are project management platform vendor discounts available for non-profits? Yes, most major platforms offer non-profit and educational discounts. Asana offers 50% off for eligible non-profits. Atlassian offers free or discounted licenses for non-profits through its community program. Monday.com offers discounts through TechSoup.

author: StackPilot Team
